checks.finding: REQ-010 and AC-006 require eight question-bank categories but never name those categories, while REQ-012 and the Invalid feature slug behavioral contract require an explicit feature mode and stable error without defining the invocation form, accepted slug grammar, or diagnostic. Two implementers could therefore produce materially different categories, command interfaces, and validation behavior, leaving task authors and verifiers unable to derive one expected product contract. These are missing product behaviors in the Phase 1 specification, not implementation-policy choices. Major is justified because the ambiguity affects user-visible CLI compatibility and required content across multiple artifacts.

The requirements, non-goals, edge cases, acceptance criteria, and behavioral contracts consistently preserve repository-only preflight and LITE behavior while adding explicit full-profile layer generation and validation; no direct conflict or impossible acceptance criterion is present.

The Edge Cases require bugfix/refactor layers to use `N/A — no change` while always assessing security, clean skipping when no mockup is supplied, and compatibility for pre-v1.4 spec directories, but the acceptance-test matrix and behavioral contracts provide no observable test for these three cases. A verifier cannot determine whether those required boundary behaviors were implemented, and task authors cannot assign complete verification work. This belongs to specification review because observable acceptance coverage is absent, rather than merely awaiting test implementation. Major is justified because the uncovered cases govern backward compatibility and whether required layer/security work may be incorrectly skipped.

The Assumptions state that current review loops can read additional canonical artifacts once their allowed-input manifests are extended or validated, and Risks say tests must prove the intended allowed-input behavior, yet no requirement or acceptance test decides whether manifests are extended, which layer artifacts become inputs, or which existing review stages consume them. An implementer must invent scope and a verifier has no expected manifest contract. This unresolved decision belongs in the specification because it determines required cross-plugin behavior before design or task decomposition. Major is justified because choosing differently can either reject the new canonical artifacts or expose unintended inputs at review boundaries.

AC-009 requires the interviewer to preserve review-gate ordering, and Main Workflows lists the spec, implementation-policy, and task review gates, but neither artifact specifies the human approval transition between reviewed Draft tasks and implementation or an observable criterion that preserves it. An implementer could satisfy the listed sequence while allowing implementation without human approval, and a verifier could not reject that behavior. Because the feature explicitly changes interviewer workflow instructions, this governance boundary must be specified at the Phase 1 gate rather than invented during implementation review. Critical is justified because omitting the approval boundary can authorize implementation across an irreversible human-control boundary.

REQ-010/AC-006 leave the eight categories unnamed, REQ-012 leaves the feature-mode interface and slug grammar unspecified, and the review-loop manifest assumption has no decided behavior or acceptance surface. Consequently the implementation-policy reviewer would have to invent product-visible question-bank, CLI, and review-boundary behavior before evaluating a design. These are specification decisions rather than architecture or task details. Major is justified because the omissions block deterministic design and task decomposition across several required deliverables.
